title: China's Economy Starts 2026 on Solid Footing
content:
China’s economy has started 2026 and its new Five-Year Plan on remarkably solid footing. A surge in inbound tourism is energizing domestic consumption, with sales of goods and services increasing significantly. This broad-based recovery in the services sector is particularly noticeable in hospitality, food, culture, and sports. Furthermore, the consumer goods trade-in program has successfully encouraged millions to replace outdated products through government subsidies. To sustain this momentum, the government recently allocated a second batch of ultra-long special treasury bond funds.

On the investment front, China’s fixed assets investment reversed its previous decline, posting positive growth in the first quarter. Foreign trade also expanded, bolstered by zero-tariff treatments granted to various developing nations across the globe. Imports from more than 150 countries and regions increased as domestic consumer demand rebounded during the extended holiday season. Meanwhile, exports remained highly resilient, driven by recovering external demand and China's well-integrated industrial system. The rapid global expansion of artificial intelligence and green industries is heavily fueling demand for these smart, sustainable products.

This robust economic performance lays a strong foundation for stable growth throughout the rest of the year. However, Chinese officials warn that external uncertainties and geopolitical tensions continue to weigh on global supply chains. Despite these evolving risks, the actual GDP growth has exceeded general market expectations, landing at the upper end of forecasts. From a global perspective, this solid expansion firmly places China among the top-performing major economies.
